one_line_takeaway: Retraining a fine-tuned model on the original clean data alone can pull a faded backdoor watermark back to as high as 100% trigger accuracy when parameters stay in the same basin (small fine-tuning LR) and triggers are out-of-distribution, but this restoration trick does not work against model-extraction attacks.
key_finding: Retraining a fine-tuned model on the original clean training data alone can restore the eroded watermark WITHOUT re-exposing trigger samples, provided fine-tuning used a small learning rate (1e-4) so parameters stay inside the original basin of attraction; trigger accuracy can be reinstated to up to 100% (clearest for noise and Unrelated/SVHN triggers). Restoration is strongest for 'unrelated' triggers (few shared features with the task) and weak/unreliable for text-overlay 'Content' triggers, which can even worsen. No single scheme is consistently most persistent across trigger types, LRs, and labeling; single-label triggers are more persistent than multi-label. Crucially, restoration FAILS against model extraction (retraining does not recover the watermark, because the surrogate lives in a different loss geometry). Blending DTRAIN into fine-tuning helps only in some ResNet cases (notably EWE) and does not help for ViT.
core_contribution: This is an empirical evaluation paper on the persistence of backdoor-based DNN watermarks under fine-tuning, plus a novel restoration idea. Its OWN contributions (Sec. I bullets, Sec. III-IV) are: (1) Watermark restoration via retraining with the ORIGINAL clean training data only, without re-exposing the secret trigger set, exploiting the 'basins of attraction' property of local minima so that parameters slide back toward the watermarked optimum; the authors claim this is the first work to use retraining for watermark restoration. (2) A loss-landscape analysis (filter-normalized contours + PCA trajectory projection, following Li et al. [40]) that visualizes how retraining steers parameters back toward the low-trigger-loss basin. (3) A fine-tuning-with-mixed-data scheme that blends a batch of owner training data every M fine-tuning batches to reduce watermark vanishing while keeping the trigger set secret from clients. Note: the five watermark schemes it evaluates (Adi, ROWBACK, Certified, EWE, APP) and the many schemes in Sec. II-B (Uchida, DeepSigns, Frontier Stitching, CosWM, UBW, etc.) are PRIOR WORK being benchmarked/reviewed, NOT this paper's inventions.
threat_model: Two IP-theft scenarios plus a model-extraction extension. (1) Ownership reclamation: an adversary steals a watermarked model, fine-tunes it on a same-domain custom dataset (fine-tuning treated as the removal attack), and redeploys it as a public service. The owner, with only API/black-box access to the suspect model plus fine-tune-as-a-service, retrains it on the original training data and checks trigger accuracy on the secret trigger set to claim ownership (trigger accuracy improves only for a previously marked model). (2) Authorized-client fine-tuning: a protocol where the owner ships the watermarked model plus a slice of training data; the client fine-tunes with an owner-defined pipeline that blends that data to keep both the trigger set (from the client) and the client's proprietary data (from the owner) private. (3) Model extraction: adversary knows the victim architecture and has access to the training inputs, trains a surrogate on victim hard-label outputs. The defender's asset is the trained DNN as intellectual property; the adversary aims to erase the ownership watermark while preserving main-task utility.
limitations: Only same-domain fine-tuning is studied (DTRAIN and DFINETUNE assumed same distribution / all layers evaluated); cross-domain transfer learning with last-layer reconfiguration is explicitly out of scope. Single dataset (CIFAR-10) and only two architectures (ResNet-18, ViT-S). Restoration works only when parameters stay in the basin, i.e., low fine-tuning LR; at medium/large LR the recovery is unclear or oscillatory. ROWBACK and EWE were not evaluated on ViT. The loss-landscape trajectory relies on PCA projection whose approximation is accurate only for checkpoints near the trajectory end. No theoretical guarantee is provided (the authors frame it as an empirical/open problem), and the blending mitigation gives only marginal, inconsistent gains. The restoration defense also assumes cooperative fine-tune-as-a-service infrastructure for the owner to retrain the suspect model.

datasets_used: CIFAR-10 (main classification task; 50K training images, 10 classes; 200 samples drawn as trigger set, remainder split 70% pretrain / 30% fine-tune)
SVHN / Street View House Numbers (source of 200 out-of-distribution 'unrelated' trigger images)
quantitative_results: Post-embedding watermark (trigger) accuracy is essentially 100% for all five schemes across trigger types on ResNet-18 (Table II), e.g., Adi/Certified/APP 100% WM acc with test acc ~87-88%
Clean (non-watermarked) baseline test accuracy: 87.86% for ResNet-18 and 77.06% for ViT-S
EWE has the lowest ResNet-18 test accuracy (~82.1%) due to its task/entanglement trade-off; ROWBACK ~85%
Restoration via retraining on DTRAIN only can reinstate trigger accuracy to up to 100% under small fine-tuning LR (1e-4), clearest for noise and unrelated triggers
Model-extraction watermark accuracy on ResNet (Table III): EWE best - Noise 65.50%, Unrelated 69.00%, FGSM 65.00%; all other schemes ~0% on Noise (Adi/Certified/APP 0.00%)
Model-extraction watermark accuracy on ViT (Table III): APP best - Unrelated 96.50%, FGSM 73.50%, Noise 47.00%; Certified Unrelated 87.50%
Retraining after model extraction did NOT improve watermark accuracy over 30 epochs (qualitative, Supp. Fig. 4)
baselines_compared: Adi et al. (USENIX Sec 2018) - abstract-image backdoor watermark
ROWBACK (Chattopadhyay & Chattopadhyay, ICMLA 2021) - FGSM triggers with uniform per-layer watermark distribution
Certified watermark (Bansal et al., ICML 2022) - randomized-smoothing certified robustness
EWE / Entangled Watermark Embedding (Jia et al., USENIX Sec 2021) - extraction-resistant entanglement
APP / Adversarial Parametric Perturbation (Gan et al., ICCV 2023) - minimax parametric-vulnerability reduction
